[[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ōsumi Province]] {{commons category|Yaku-jinja}} [[JA:益救神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11581207}} [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] {{translated page|ja|益救神社|version=107360945|comment=Imported full ja history}}[[Category:Automerged Japanese text]] == Japanese Wikipedia content == {{神社 |名称 = 益救神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Yaku Shrine Haiden.jpg|270px|拝殿]]
拝殿 |所在地 = {{jalink|鹿児島県|Q15701}}{{jalink|熊毛郡 (鹿児島県)|Q1132154|熊毛郡}}{{jalink|屋久島町|Q1350724}}宮之浦字水洗尻277番地 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|30|25|37.70|N|130|34|18.52|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=益救神社}} |祭神 = {{jalink|ホオリ|Q1051960|天津日高彦火火出見尊}} |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（小）
旧{{jalink|県社}} |創建 = 不詳 |本殿 = 三間社{{jalink|流造|Q210684}} |別名 =御嶽宮（三岳宮）
須久比宮
一品宝珠大権現 |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|4月29日|Q2535}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kagoshima|label=益救神社|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Yaku Shrine Torii.jpg|サムネイル|鳥居]] [[ファイル:Yaku jinja shrine.jpg|サムネイル|本殿]] '''益救神社'''（やくじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|鹿児島県|Q15701}}{{jalink|熊毛郡 (鹿児島県)|Q1132154|熊毛郡}}{{jalink|屋久島町|Q1350724}}（{{jalink|屋久島|Q242176}}）にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}}で、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は{{jalink|県社}}。{{jalink|多禰国|Q1370581}}の{{jalink|一宮|Q1656379}}。 『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}』{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|神名帳}}に記載された神社のうち最も南に位置する。現在は「やく」と読むが、かつては「ますくひ」「すくひ」と読んだ。旧称は「須久比ノ宮」「一品宝珠大権現」。 == 祭神 == === 正祀 === * 天津日高彦火火出見尊 （あまつひこひこほほでみのみこと） *: {{jalink|山幸彦と海幸彦|Q11468336|海幸山幸神話}}に登場する「山幸彦」。 === 配祀 === * {{jalink|オオヤマツミ|Q386563|大山祇尊}} * {{jalink|コノハナノサクヤビメ|Q1781862|木花開耶姫命尊}} * {{jalink|シオツチノオジ||塩土翁尊}} * {{jalink|ワタツミ|Q1474187|豊玉彦命尊}} * {{jalink|トヨタマヒメ|Q2329261|豊玉姫尊}} * {{jalink|タマヨリビメ (日向神話)|Q3082751|玉依姫尊}} 元々は屋久島中央部の三岳（{{jalink|宮之浦岳|Q10950373}}・{{jalink|永田岳|Q11550240}}・{{jalink|栗生岳|Q11536028}}）の神を祀ったものと考えられ、かつては島内各地に三岳の遥拝所があった。{{jalink|明治維新|Q8707}}までは、旧村18か所に村落名を冠した益救神社があった谷川健一編『日本の神々 -神社と聖地- 1 九州』白水社、1984年4月が、現在は宮之浦の当社を除けば原集落にある原益救神社（屋久島町 原721-2）のみとなっている。また、{{jalink|宮之浦岳|Q10950373}}の山頂には当社の奥社がある。 == 歴史 == 創建の由緒は不詳。 『{{jalink|日本書紀|Q813935}}』{{jalink|舒明天皇|Q329761}}元年（{{jalink|629年|Q33258}}）4月1日の条には{{jalink|田部連}}（たべのむらじ）を掖玖（やく、屋久島）に遣わしたとの記事がある。『日本書紀』{{jalink|天武天皇|Q335181}}8年（{{jalink|679年|Q23451}}）11月23日の条にある{{jalink|種子島|Q696228}}へ使者を遣わした記述と比べ、屋久島への使者が50年も早いことから、屋久島が南海航路の目標として重要だったことを裏付けていると考えられている。さらに、『{{jalink|続日本紀|Q611399}}』{{jalink|天平勝宝|Q1373169}}6年（{{jalink|754年|Q23646}}）2月20日の条では、{{jalink|天平|Q1199879}}7年（{{jalink|735年|Q23576}}）に{{jalink|高橋連牛養}}（たかはしのむらじうしかひ）を南島に遣わし、島名・港・湧水地などを記した牌を建てさせて{{jalink|遣唐使|Q262529|遣唐使船}}の漂着に備えさせたと記述されており、当社の別名「スクイ」は、益救（屋久）の救の字の訓を誤ったものとの見方八田知紀ほか『薩隅日地理纂考』鹿児島県私立教育会、1898年。がある一方で、この牌すなわち{{jalink|澪標|Q11566019}}（みおつくし）の「ツクシ」が訛ったもので、当社の機能的由来をよく表した語だという『式内社調査報告』の説もある。 {{jalink|8世紀|Q8086}}から{{jalink|9世紀|Q8083}}初頭にかけて屋久島と種子島が{{jalink|多禰国|Q1370581}}であった時代は、当社がその{{jalink|一宮|Q1656379}}であったと伝えられる。{{jalink|延長 (元号)|Q1327274|延長}}5年（{{jalink|927年|Q30470}}）成立の『延喜式』神名帳では{{jalink|大隅国|Q981002}}{{jalink|馭謨郡|Q11668133}}に「益救神社」と記載されたが、これが当社の文献上における初出とされている。これにより小社に列し、また最も南にある式内社となった。 {{jalink|戦国時代 (日本)|Q204023|戦国時代}}、屋久島は{{jalink|種子島氏|Q2975383}}の所領となったが、種子島氏は{{jalink|法華宗|Q44733349}}を重視したことから当社は衰微し、所在も不明となった。屋久島の宗教は{{jalink|律宗|Q1351551}}であったが、{{jalink|文明 (日本)|Q1193544|文明}}元年（{{jalink|1469年|Q6566}}）ごろには法華宗になった。また、八重岳（{{jalink|永田岳|Q11550240}}）が振動し、種々の怪異が起こったことから{{jalink|長享|Q968843}}2年（{{jalink|1488年|Q4853}}）に{{jalink|日増}}という僧が来島し、御岳（宮之浦岳・永田岳・{{jalink|栗生岳|Q11536028}}・{{jalink|黒味岳|Q11678042}}の4山を指す）に登って法礼を納めて鎮めたことがあり、法華宗と御岳信仰に対立があったのだろうと推察されている。 {{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}に入ると屋久島は{{jalink|薩摩藩|Q676187}}領となったが、{{jalink|貞享|Q1146221}}元年（{{jalink|1684年|Q7654}}）に宰領として赴任した薩摩藩士{{jalink|町田孫七忠以}}は益救神社の荒廃を聞いてそれを嘆き、神社の跡地を探し出して、翌貞享2年（{{jalink|1685年|Q7660}}）現在地に社殿を再建した。このとき、古記もすでに失われ、祭神は{{jalink|ホオリ|Q1051960|火火出見尊}}以外は何であったか不明になっていたので、火出見尊・{{jalink|火須勢理命|Q10731395|火須世理尊}}・{{jalink|えびす|Q1129330|恵美須神}}を主祭神とし、脇殿に現在の配祀神を祀ったとされる。{{jalink|文久|Q730266}}3年（{{jalink|1863年|Q7701}}）にも薩摩藩直営で社殿を造営し、神料50{{jalink|石 (単位)|Q855048|石}}を献じた。 {{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}4年（{{jalink|1871年|Q7746}}）に主祭神のうち火須世理尊・恵美須神を他の神社へ分祀し、現在の祭神となった。 {{jalink|第二次世界大戦|Q362}}中の{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}20年（{{jalink|1945年|Q5240}}）7月15日、{{jalink|アメリカ軍|Q11211}}の爆撃により社殿が焼失した。再建の費用捻出のため境内地の一部と境内の杉約2,000本を買却し、昭和29年（{{jalink|1954年|Q18581}}）に現在の社殿を建立している。 == 境内 == * 本殿 - {{jalink|流造|Q210684|三間社流造}}。 == 岳参り == 屋久島では、春秋の{{jalink|彼岸|Q866977}}になると、村落ごとに若者を中心とした一団が御岳に登山し、{{jalink|シャクナゲ|Q4389817}}の枝を土産として里に持って帰る風習がある。 村落ごとに登る山は違うが、いずれにせよ島中心部の標高1,800 mを越える山岳地帯へ行くには2 - 3日を要するため、代表者が山へ登った。留守の者達は前岳（自分の村落から見上げる山）まで登って代表の者たちを出迎えるか、詣所（もいしょ）と呼ばれる揺拝所に出迎え、そこで神霊ののったシャクナゲの枝を貰い、各家の床の間に飾った。しかし昭和も終わり頃になると、自然保護の観点からシャクナゲを持ち帰ることは廃れていった。 == 所在地 == * {{jalink|鹿児島県|Q15701}}{{jalink|熊毛郡 (鹿児島県)|Q1132154|熊毛郡}}{{jalink|屋久島町|Q1350724}}宮之浦字水洗尻277番地 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 外部リンク == {{commonscat}} * {{official website}} {{神道 横}} ==End Japanese== [[Category:鹿児島県の神社]] [[Category:屋久島町の建築物]] [[Category:屋久島町の歴史]] [[Category:大隅国の式内社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:鹿児島県の旧県社]]

{{nihongo|'''Yaku Shrine'''|益救神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Ōsumi Province]].

{{nihongo|'''Yaku Shrine'''|益救神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Ōsumi Province]].

|名称 = 益救神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Yaku Shrine Haiden.jpg|270px|拝殿]]
拝殿 |所在地 = {{jalink|鹿児島県|Q15701}}{{jalink|熊毛郡 (鹿児島県)|Q1132154|熊毛郡}}{{jalink|屋久島町|Q1350724}}宮之浦字水洗尻277番地 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|30|25|37.70|N|130|34|18.52|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=益救神社}} |祭神 = {{jalink|ホオリ|Q1051960|天津日高彦火火出見尊}} |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（小）
旧{{jalink|県社}} |創建 = 不詳 |本殿 = 三間社{{jalink|流造|Q210684}} |別名 =御嶽宮（三岳宮）
須久比宮
一品宝珠大権現 |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|4月29日|Q2535}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kagoshima|label=益救神社|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Yaku Shrine Torii.jpg|サムネイル|鳥居]] [[ファイル:Yaku jinja shrine.jpg|サムネイル|本殿]] '''益救神社'''（やくじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|鹿児島県|Q15701}}{{jalink|熊毛郡 (鹿児島県)|Q1132154|熊毛郡}}{{jalink|屋久島町|Q1350724}}（{{jalink|屋久島|Q242176}}）にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}}で、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は{{jalink|県社}}。{{jalink|多禰国|Q1370581}}の{{jalink|一宮|Q1656379}}。 『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}』{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|神名帳}}に記載された神社のうち最も南に位置する。現在は「やく」と読むが、かつては「ますくひ」「すくひ」と読んだ。旧称は「須久比ノ宮」「一品宝珠大権現」。 === 祭神 === === 正祀 === * 天津日高彦火火出見尊 （あまつひこひこほほでみのみこと） *: {{jalink|山幸彦と海幸彦|Q11468336|海幸山幸神話}}に登場する「山幸彦」。 === 配祀 === * {{jalink|オオヤマツミ|Q386563|大山祇尊}} * {{jalink|コノハナノサクヤビメ|Q1781862|木花開耶姫命尊}} * {{jalink|シオツチノオジ||塩土翁尊}} * {{jalink|ワタツミ|Q1474187|豊玉彦命尊}} * {{jalink|トヨタマヒメ|Q2329261|豊玉姫尊}} * {{jalink|タマヨリビメ (日向神話)|Q3082751|玉依姫尊}} 元々は屋久島中央部の三岳（{{jalink|宮之浦岳|Q10950373}}・{{jalink|永田岳|Q11550240}}・{{jalink|栗生岳|Q11536028}}）の神を祀ったものと考えられ、かつては島内各地に三岳の遥拝所があった。{{jalink|明治維新|Q8707}}までは、旧村18か所に村落名を冠した益救神社があった谷川健一編『日本の神々 -神社と聖地- 1 九州』白水社、1984年4月が、現在は宮之浦の当社を除けば原集落にある原益救神社（屋久島町 原721-2）のみとなっている。また、{{jalink|宮之浦岳|Q10950373}}の山頂には当社の奥社がある。 === 歴史 === 創建の由緒は不詳。 『{{jalink|日本書紀|Q813935}}』{{jalink|舒明天皇|Q329761}}元年（{{jalink|629年|Q33258}}）4月1日の条には{{jalink|田部連}}（たべのむらじ）を掖玖（やく、屋久島）に遣わしたとの記事がある。『日本書紀』{{jalink|天武天皇|Q335181}}8年（{{jalink|679年|Q23451}}）11月23日の条にある{{jalink|種子島|Q696228}}へ使者を遣わした記述と比べ、屋久島への使者が50年も早いことから、屋久島が南海航路の目標として重要だったことを裏付けていると考えられている。さらに、『{{jalink|続日本紀|Q611399}}』{{jalink|天平勝宝|Q1373169}}6年（{{jalink|754年|Q23646}}）2月20日の条では、{{jalink|天平|Q1199879}}7年（{{jalink|735年|Q23576}}）に{{jalink|高橋連牛養}}（たかはしのむらじうしかひ）を南島に遣わし、島名・港・湧水地などを記した牌を建てさせて{{jalink|遣唐使|Q262529|遣唐使船}}の漂着に備えさせたと記述されており、当社の別名「スクイ」は、益救（屋久）の救の字の訓を誤ったものとの見方八田知紀ほか『薩隅日地理纂考』鹿児島県私立教育会、1898年。がある一方で、この牌すなわち{{jalink|澪標|Q11566019}}（みおつくし）の「ツクシ」が訛ったもので、当社の機能的由来をよく表した語だという『式内社調査報告』の説もある。 {{jalink|8世紀|Q8086}}から{{jalink|9世紀|Q8083}}初頭にかけて屋久島と種子島が{{jalink|多禰国|Q1370581}}であった時代は、当社がその{{jalink|一宮|Q1656379}}であったと伝えられる。{{jalink|延長 (元号)|Q1327274|延長}}5年（{{jalink|927年|Q30470}}）成立の『延喜式』神名帳では{{jalink|大隅国|Q981002}}{{jalink|馭謨郡|Q11668133}}に「益救神社」と記載されたが、これが当社の文献上における初出とされている。これにより小社に列し、また最も南にある式内社となった。 {{jalink|戦国時代 (日本)|Q204023|戦国時代}}、屋久島は{{jalink|種子島氏|Q2975383}}の所領となったが、種子島氏は{{jalink|法華宗|Q44733349}}を重視したことから当社は衰微し、所在も不明となった。屋久島の宗教は{{jalink|律宗|Q1351551}}であったが、{{jalink|文明 (日本)|Q1193544|文明}}元年（{{jalink|1469年|Q6566}}）ごろには法華宗になった。また、八重岳（{{jalink|永田岳|Q11550240}}）が振動し、種々の怪異が起こったことから{{jalink|長享|Q968843}}2年（{{jalink|1488年|Q4853}}）に{{jalink|日増}}という僧が来島し、御岳（宮之浦岳・永田岳・{{jalink|栗生岳|Q11536028}}・{{jalink|黒味岳|Q11678042}}の4山を指す）に登って法礼を納めて鎮めたことがあり、法華宗と御岳信仰に対立があったのだろうと推察されている。 {{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}に入ると屋久島は{{jalink|薩摩藩|Q676187}}領となったが、{{jalink|貞享|Q1146221}}元年（{{jalink|1684年|Q7654}}）に宰領として赴任した薩摩藩士{{jalink|町田孫七忠以}}は益救神社の荒廃を聞いてそれを嘆き、神社の跡地を探し出して、翌貞享2年（{{jalink|1685年|Q7660}}）現在地に社殿を再建した。このとき、古記もすでに失われ、祭神は{{jalink|ホオリ|Q1051960|火火出見尊}}以外は何であったか不明になっていたので、火出見尊・{{jalink|火須勢理命|Q10731395|火須世理尊}}・{{jalink|えびす|Q1129330|恵美須神}}を主祭神とし、脇殿に現在の配祀神を祀ったとされる。{{jalink|文久|Q730266}}3年（{{jalink|1863年|Q7701}}）にも薩摩藩直営で社殿を造営し、神料50{{jalink|石 (単位)|Q855048|石}}を献じた。 {{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}4年（{{jalink|1871年|Q7746}}）に主祭神のうち火須世理尊・恵美須神を他の神社へ分祀し、現在の祭神となった。 {{jalink|第二次世界大戦|Q362}}中の{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}20年（{{jalink|1945年|Q5240}}）7月15日、{{jalink|アメリカ軍|Q11211}}の爆撃により社殿が焼失した。再建の費用捻出のため境内地の一部と境内の杉約2,000本を買却し、昭和29年（{{jalink|1954年|Q18581}}）に現在の社殿を建立している。 === 境内 === * 本殿 - {{jalink|流造|Q210684|三間社流造}}。 === 岳参り === 屋久島では、春秋の{{jalink|彼岸|Q866977}}になると、村落ごとに若者を中心とした一団が御岳に登山し、{{jalink|シャクナゲ|Q4389817}}の枝を土産として里に持って帰る風習がある。 村落ごとに登る山は違うが、いずれにせよ島中心部の標高1,800 mを越える山岳地帯へ行くには2 - 3日を要するため、代表者が山へ登った。留守の者達は前岳（自分の村落から見上げる山）まで登って代表の者たちを出迎えるか、詣所（もいしょ）と呼ばれる揺拝所に出迎え、そこで神霊ののったシャクナゲの枝を貰い、各家の床の間に飾った。しかし昭和も終わり頃になると、自然保護の観点からシャクナゲを持ち帰ることは廃れていった。 === 所在地 === * {{jalink|鹿児島県|Q15701}}{{jalink|熊毛郡 (鹿児島県)|Q1132154|熊毛郡}}{{jalink|屋久島町|Q1350724}}宮之浦字水洗尻277番地 === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === 外部リンク === {{commonscat}} * {{official website}} {{神道 横}} Yaku-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '鹿児島県熊毛郡屋久島町宮之浦277', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Yaku-jinja}} [[JA:益救神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11581207}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 益救神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Yaku Shrine Haiden.jpg|270px|拝殿]]
拝殿 |所在地 = {{jalink|鹿児島県|Q15701}}{{jalink|熊毛郡 (鹿児島県)|Q1132154|熊毛郡}}{{jalink|屋久島町|Q1350724}}宮之浦字水洗尻277番地 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|30|25|37.70|N|130|34|18.52|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=益救神社}} |祭神 = {{jalink|ホオリ|Q1051960|天津日高彦火火出見尊}} |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（小）
旧{{jalink|県社}} |創建 = 不詳 |本殿 = 三間社{{jalink|流造|Q210684}} |別名 =御嶽宮（三岳宮）
須久比宮
一品宝珠大権現 |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|4月29日|Q2535}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kagoshima|label=益救神社|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Yaku Shrine Torii.jpg|サムネイル|鳥居]] [[ファイル:Yaku jinja shrine.jpg|サムネイル|本殿]] '''益救神社'''（やくじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|鹿児島県|Q15701}}{{jalink|熊毛郡 (鹿児島県)|Q1132154|熊毛郡}}{{jalink|屋久島町|Q1350724}}（{{jalink|屋久島|Q242176}}）にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}}で、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は{{jalink|県社}}。{{jalink|多禰国|Q1370581}}の{{jalink|一宮|Q1656379}}。 『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}』{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|神名帳}}に記載された神社のうち最も南に位置する。現在は「やく」と読むが、かつては「ますくひ」「すくひ」と読んだ。旧称は「須久比ノ宮」「一品宝珠大権現」。 === 祭神 === === 正祀 === * 天津日高彦火火出見尊 （あまつひこひこほほでみのみこと） *: {{jalink|山幸彦と海幸彦|Q11468336|海幸山幸神話}}に登場する「山幸彦」。 === 配祀 === * {{jalink|オオヤマツミ|Q386563|大山祇尊}} * {{jalink|コノハナノサクヤビメ|Q1781862|木花開耶姫命尊}} * {{jalink|シオツチノオジ||塩土翁尊}} * {{jalink|ワタツミ|Q1474187|豊玉彦命尊}} * {{jalink|トヨタマヒメ|Q2329261|豊玉姫尊}} * {{jalink|タマヨリビメ (日向神話)|Q3082751|玉依姫尊}} 元々は屋久島中央部の三岳（{{jalink|宮之浦岳|Q10950373}}・{{jalink|永田岳|Q11550240}}・{{jalink|栗生岳|Q11536028}}）の神を祀ったものと考えられ、かつては島内各地に三岳の遥拝所があった。{{jalink|明治維新|Q8707}}までは、旧村18か所に村落名を冠した益救神社があった谷川健一編『日本の神々 -神社と聖地- 1 九州』白水社、1984年4月が、現在は宮之浦の当社を除けば原集落にある原益救神社（屋久島町 原721-2）のみとなっている。また、{{jalink|宮之浦岳|Q10950373}}の山頂には当社の奥社がある。 === 歴史 === 創建の由緒は不詳。 『{{jalink|日本書紀|Q813935}}』{{jalink|舒明天皇|Q329761}}元年（{{jalink|629年|Q33258}}）4月1日の条には{{jalink|田部連}}（たべのむらじ）を掖玖（やく、屋久島）に遣わしたとの記事がある。『日本書紀』{{jalink|天武天皇|Q335181}}8年（{{jalink|679年|Q23451}}）11月23日の条にある{{jalink|種子島|Q696228}}へ使者を遣わした記述と比べ、屋久島への使者が50年も早いことから、屋久島が南海航路の目標として重要だったことを裏付けていると考えられている。さらに、『{{jalink|続日本紀|Q611399}}』{{jalink|天平勝宝|Q1373169}}6年（{{jalink|754年|Q23646}}）2月20日の条では、{{jalink|天平|Q1199879}}7年（{{jalink|735年|Q23576}}）に{{jalink|高橋連牛養}}（たかはしのむらじうしかひ）を南島に遣わし、島名・港・湧水地などを記した牌を建てさせて{{jalink|遣唐使|Q262529|遣唐使船}}の漂着に備えさせたと記述されており、当社の別名「スクイ」は、益救（屋久）の救の字の訓を誤ったものとの見方八田知紀ほか『薩隅日地理纂考』鹿児島県私立教育会、1898年。がある一方で、この牌すなわち{{jalink|澪標|Q11566019}}（みおつくし）の「ツクシ」が訛ったもので、当社の機能的由来をよく表した語だという『式内社調査報告』の説もある。 {{jalink|8世紀|Q8086}}から{{jalink|9世紀|Q8083}}初頭にかけて屋久島と種子島が{{jalink|多禰国|Q1370581}}であった時代は、当社がその{{jalink|一宮|Q1656379}}であったと伝えられる。{{jalink|延長 (元号)|Q1327274|延長}}5年（{{jalink|927年|Q30470}}）成立の『延喜式』神名帳では{{jalink|大隅国|Q981002}}{{jalink|馭謨郡|Q11668133}}に「益救神社」と記載されたが、これが当社の文献上における初出とされている。これにより小社に列し、また最も南にある式内社となった。 {{jalink|戦国時代 (日本)|Q204023|戦国時代}}、屋久島は{{jalink|種子島氏|Q2975383}}の所領となったが、種子島氏は{{jalink|法華宗|Q44733349}}を重視したことから当社は衰微し、所在も不明となった。屋久島の宗教は{{jalink|律宗|Q1351551}}であったが、{{jalink|文明 (日本)|Q1193544|文明}}元年（{{jalink|1469年|Q6566}}）ごろには法華宗になった。また、八重岳（{{jalink|永田岳|Q11550240}}）が振動し、種々の怪異が起こったことから{{jalink|長享|Q968843}}2年（{{jalink|1488年|Q4853}}）に{{jalink|日増}}という僧が来島し、御岳（宮之浦岳・永田岳・{{jalink|栗生岳|Q11536028}}・{{jalink|黒味岳|Q11678042}}の4山を指す）に登って法礼を納めて鎮めたことがあり、法華宗と御岳信仰に対立があったのだろうと推察されている。 {{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}に入ると屋久島は{{jalink|薩摩藩|Q676187}}領となったが、{{jalink|貞享|Q1146221}}元年（{{jalink|1684年|Q7654}}）に宰領として赴任した薩摩藩士{{jalink|町田孫七忠以}}は益救神社の荒廃を聞いてそれを嘆き、神社の跡地を探し出して、翌貞享2年（{{jalink|1685年|Q7660}}）現在地に社殿を再建した。このとき、古記もすでに失われ、祭神は{{jalink|ホオリ|Q1051960|火火出見尊}}以外は何であったか不明になっていたので、火出見尊・{{jalink|火須勢理命|Q10731395|火須世理尊}}・{{jalink|えびす|Q1129330|恵美須神}}を主祭神とし、脇殿に現在の配祀神を祀ったとされる。{{jalink|文久|Q730266}}3年（{{jalink|1863年|Q7701}}）にも薩摩藩直営で社殿を造営し、神料50{{jalink|石 (単位)|Q855048|石}}を献じた。 {{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}4年（{{jalink|1871年|Q7746}}）に主祭神のうち火須世理尊・恵美須神を他の神社へ分祀し、現在の祭神となった。 {{jalink|第二次世界大戦|Q362}}中の{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}20年（{{jalink|1945年|Q5240}}）7月15日、{{jalink|アメリカ軍|Q11211}}の爆撃により社殿が焼失した。再建の費用捻出のため境内地の一部と境内の杉約2,000本を買却し、昭和29年（{{jalink|1954年|Q18581}}）に現在の社殿を建立している。 === 境内 === * 本殿 - {{jalink|流造|Q210684|三間社流造}}。 === 岳参り === 屋久島では、春秋の{{jalink|彼岸|Q866977}}になると、村落ごとに若者を中心とした一団が御岳に登山し、{{jalink|シャクナゲ|Q4389817}}の枝を土産として里に持って帰る風習がある。 村落ごとに登る山は違うが、いずれにせよ島中心部の標高1,800 mを越える山岳地帯へ行くには2 - 3日を要するため、代表者が山へ登った。留守の者達は前岳（自分の村落から見上げる山）まで登って代表の者たちを出迎えるか、詣所（もいしょ）と呼ばれる揺拝所に出迎え、そこで神霊ののったシャクナゲの枝を貰い、各家の床の間に飾った。しかし昭和も終わり頃になると、自然保護の観点からシャクナゲを持ち帰ることは廃れていった。 === 所在地 === * {{jalink|鹿児島県|Q15701}}{{jalink|熊毛郡 (鹿児島県)|Q1132154|熊毛郡}}{{jalink|屋久島町|Q1350724}}宮之浦字水洗尻277番地 === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === 外部リンク === {{commonscat}} * {{official website}} {{神道 横}} |名称 = 益救神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Yaku Shrine Haiden.jpg|270px|拝殿]]
拝殿 |所在地 = {{jalink|鹿児島県|Q15701}}{{jalink|熊毛郡 (鹿児島県)|Q1132154|熊毛郡}}{{jalink|屋久島町|Q1350724}}宮之浦字水洗尻277番地 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|30|25|37.70|N|130|34|18.52|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=益救神社}} |祭神 = {{jalink|ホオリ|Q1051960|天津日高彦火火出見尊}} |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（小）
旧{{jalink|県社}} |創建 = 不詳 |本殿 = 三間社{{jalink|流造|Q210684}} |別名 =御嶽宮（三岳宮）
須久比宮
一品宝珠大権現 |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|4月29日|Q2535}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kagoshima|label=益救神社|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Yaku Shrine Torii.jpg|サムネイル|鳥居]] [[ファイル:Yaku jinja shrine.jpg|サムネイル|本殿]] '''益救神社'''（やくじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|鹿児島県|Q15701}}{{jalink|熊毛郡 (鹿児島県)|Q1132154|熊毛郡}}{{jalink|屋久島町|Q1350724}}（{{jalink|屋久島|Q242176}}）にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}}で、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は{{jalink|県社}}。{{jalink|多禰国|Q1370581}}の{{jalink|一宮|Q1656379}}。 『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}』{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|神名帳}}に記載された神社のうち最も南に位置する。現在は「やく」と読むが、かつては「ますくひ」「すくひ」と読んだ。旧称は「須久比ノ宮」「一品宝珠大権現」。 === 祭神 === === 正祀 === * 天津日高彦火火出見尊 （あまつひこひこほほでみのみこと） *: {{jalink|山幸彦と海幸彦|Q11468336|海幸山幸神話}}に登場する「山幸彦」。 === 配祀 === * {{jalink|オオヤマツミ|Q386563|大山祇尊}} * {{jalink|コノハナノサクヤビメ|Q1781862|木花開耶姫命尊}} * {{jalink|シオツチノオジ||塩土翁尊}} * {{jalink|ワタツミ|Q1474187|豊玉彦命尊}} * {{jalink|トヨタマヒメ|Q2329261|豊玉姫尊}} * {{jalink|タマヨリビメ (日向神話)|Q3082751|玉依姫尊}} 元々は屋久島中央部の三岳（{{jalink|宮之浦岳|Q10950373}}・{{jalink|永田岳|Q11550240}}・{{jalink|栗生岳|Q11536028}}）の神を祀ったものと考えられ、かつては島内各地に三岳の遥拝所があった。{{jalink|明治維新|Q8707}}までは、旧村18か所に村落名を冠した益救神社があった谷川健一編『日本の神々 -神社と聖地- 1 九州』白水社、1984年4月が、現在は宮之浦の当社を除けば原集落にある原益救神社（屋久島町 原721-2）のみとなっている。また、{{jalink|宮之浦岳|Q10950373}}の山頂には当社の奥社がある。 === 歴史 === 創建の由緒は不詳。 『{{jalink|日本書紀|Q813935}}』{{jalink|舒明天皇|Q329761}}元年（{{jalink|629年|Q33258}}）4月1日の条には{{jalink|田部連}}（たべのむらじ）を掖玖（やく、屋久島）に遣わしたとの記事がある。『日本書紀』{{jalink|天武天皇|Q335181}}8年（{{jalink|679年|Q23451}}）11月23日の条にある{{jalink|種子島|Q696228}}へ使者を遣わした記述と比べ、屋久島への使者が50年も早いことから、屋久島が南海航路の目標として重要だったことを裏付けていると考えられている。さらに、『{{jalink|続日本紀|Q611399}}』{{jalink|天平勝宝|Q1373169}}6年（{{jalink|754年|Q23646}}）2月20日の条では、{{jalink|天平|Q1199879}}7年（{{jalink|735年|Q23576}}）に{{jalink|高橋連牛養}}（たかはしのむらじうしかひ）を南島に遣わし、島名・港・湧水地などを記した牌を建てさせて{{jalink|遣唐使|Q262529|遣唐使船}}の漂着に備えさせたと記述されており、当社の別名「スクイ」は、益救（屋久）の救の字の訓を誤ったものとの見方八田知紀ほか『薩隅日地理纂考』鹿児島県私立教育会、1898年。がある一方で、この牌すなわち{{jalink|澪標|Q11566019}}（みおつくし）の「ツクシ」が訛ったもので、当社の機能的由来をよく表した語だという『式内社調査報告』の説もある。 {{jalink|8世紀|Q8086}}から{{jalink|9世紀|Q8083}}初頭にかけて屋久島と種子島が{{jalink|多禰国|Q1370581}}であった時代は、当社がその{{jalink|一宮|Q1656379}}であったと伝えられる。{{jalink|延長 (元号)|Q1327274|延長}}5年（{{jalink|927年|Q30470}}）成立の『延喜式』神名帳では{{jalink|大隅国|Q981002}}{{jalink|馭謨郡|Q11668133}}に「益救神社」と記載されたが、これが当社の文献上における初出とされている。これにより小社に列し、また最も南にある式内社となった。 {{jalink|戦国時代 (日本)|Q204023|戦国時代}}、屋久島は{{jalink|種子島氏|Q2975383}}の所領となったが、種子島氏は{{jalink|法華宗|Q44733349}}を重視したことから当社は衰微し、所在も不明となった。屋久島の宗教は{{jalink|律宗|Q1351551}}であったが、{{jalink|文明 (日本)|Q1193544|文明}}元年（{{jalink|1469年|Q6566}}）ごろには法華宗になった。また、八重岳（{{jalink|永田岳|Q11550240}}）が振動し、種々の怪異が起こったことから{{jalink|長享|Q968843}}2年（{{jalink|1488年|Q4853}}）に{{jalink|日増}}という僧が来島し、御岳（宮之浦岳・永田岳・{{jalink|栗生岳|Q11536028}}・{{jalink|黒味岳|Q11678042}}の4山を指す）に登って法礼を納めて鎮めたことがあり、法華宗と御岳信仰に対立があったのだろうと推察されている。 {{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}に入ると屋久島は{{jalink|薩摩藩|Q676187}}領となったが、{{jalink|貞享|Q1146221}}元年（{{jalink|1684年|Q7654}}）に宰領として赴任した薩摩藩士{{jalink|町田孫七忠以}}は益救神社の荒廃を聞いてそれを嘆き、神社の跡地を探し出して、翌貞享2年（{{jalink|1685年|Q7660}}）現在地に社殿を再建した。このとき、古記もすでに失われ、祭神は{{jalink|ホオリ|Q1051960|火火出見尊}}以外は何であったか不明になっていたので、火出見尊・{{jalink|火須勢理命|Q10731395|火須世理尊}}・{{jalink|えびす|Q1129330|恵美須神}}を主祭神とし、脇殿に現在の配祀神を祀ったとされる。{{jalink|文久|Q730266}}3年（{{jalink|1863年|Q7701}}）にも薩摩藩直営で社殿を造営し、神料50{{jalink|石 (単位)|Q855048|石}}を献じた。 {{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}4年（{{jalink|1871年|Q7746}}）に主祭神のうち火須世理尊・恵美須神を他の神社へ分祀し、現在の祭神となった。 {{jalink|第二次世界大戦|Q362}}中の{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}20年（{{jalink|1945年|Q5240}}）7月15日、{{jalink|アメリカ軍|Q11211}}の爆撃により社殿が焼失した。再建の費用捻出のため境内地の一部と境内の杉約2,000本を買却し、昭和29年（{{jalink|1954年|Q18581}}）に現在の社殿を建立している。 === 境内 === * 本殿 - {{jalink|流造|Q210684|三間社流造}}。 === 岳参り === 屋久島では、春秋の{{jalink|彼岸|Q866977}}になると、村落ごとに若者を中心とした一団が御岳に登山し、{{jalink|シャクナゲ|Q4389817}}の枝を土産として里に持って帰る風習がある。 村落ごとに登る山は違うが、いずれにせよ島中心部の標高1,800 mを越える山岳地帯へ行くには2 - 3日を要するため、代表者が山へ登った。留守の者達は前岳（自分の村落から見上げる山）まで登って代表の者たちを出迎えるか、詣所（もいしょ）と呼ばれる揺拝所に出迎え、そこで神霊ののったシャクナゲの枝を貰い、各家の床の間に飾った。しかし昭和も終わり頃になると、自然保護の観点からシャクナゲを持ち帰ることは廃れていった。 === 所在地 === * {{jalink|鹿児島県|Q15701}}{{jalink|熊毛郡 (鹿児島県)|Q1132154|熊毛郡}}{{jalink|屋久島町|Q1350724}}宮之浦字水洗尻277番地 === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === 外部リンク === {{commonscat}} * {{official website}} {{神道 横}} {{Shrine |Name = Yaku Shrine |Image = [[File:Yaku Shrine Haiden.jpg|270px|Haiden]]
Haiden |Location = {{jalink|Kagoshima Prefecture|Q15701}}{{jalink|Kumage District (Kagoshima Prefecture)|Q1132154|Kumage District}}{{jalink|Yakushima Town|Q1350724}} Miyano-ura aza Mizuarai-jiri 277 |Coordinates = {{Wikicoord2dms|30|25|37.70|N|130|34|18.52|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yaku Shrine}} |Deity = {{jalink|Hoori|Q1051960|Amatsu-Hikohiko Hohodemi no Mikoto}} |Rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (Minor)
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = Three-bay {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Other Names = Mitake-gū (Mitsudake-gū)
Sukui-gū
Ippin Hōju Daigongen |Pilgrimage Sites = |Annual Festival = {{jalink|April 29|Q2535}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagoshima|label=Yaku Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Yaku Shrine Torii.jpg|Thumbnail|Torii]] [[File:Yaku jinja shrine.jpg|Thumbnail|Main hall]] '''Yaku Shrine''' (やくじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in {{jalink|Yakushima Town|Q1350724}} ({{jalink|Yakushima|Q242176}}), {{jalink|Kumage District (Kagoshima Prefecture)|Q1132154|Kumage District}}, {{jalink|Kagoshima Prefecture|Q15701}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and under the {{jalink|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}} it was ranked as a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. It is the {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Tane Province|Q1370581}}. Among the shrines listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}} {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}, it is the southernmost. Today it is read “Yaku,” but in the past it was read “Masukui” or “Sukui.” Former names include “Sukui-no-miya” and “Ippin Hōju Daigongen.” === Enshrined Deities === === Principal Deity === * Amatsu-Hikohiko Hohodemi no Mikoto (あまつひこひこほほでみのみこと) *: Known as “Yamasachihiko” in the {{jalink|Myth of Yamasachihiko and Umasachihiko|Q11468336|Sea and Mountain Luck myth}}. === Associated Deities === * {{jalink|Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|Konohana-Sakuya-Hime no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Shiotsuchi no Oji||Shiotsuchi no Oji no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Watatsumi|Q1474187|Toyotama-hiko no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Toyotama-hime|Q2329261|Toyotama-hime no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Tamayori-hime (Hyūga mythology)|Q3082751|Tamayori-hime no Mikoto}} It is thought that the shrine originally enshrined the deities of the three central mountains of Yakushima—{{jalink|Miyanoura-dake|Q10950373}}, {{jalink|Nagata-dake|Q11550240}}, and {{jalink|Kurio-dake|Q11536028}}—and in the past there were distant worship sites for these three mountains throughout the island. Until the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, there were Yaku Shrines bearing the names of their villages in 18 former villagesEdited by Ken’ichi Tanigawa, *The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Vol. 1 Kyushu*, Hakusuisha, April 1984., but today only this shrine in Miyano-ura and the Hara Yaku Shrine in the Hara settlement (Yakushima Town, Hara 721-2) remain. In addition, an inner shrine of this shrine stands on the summit of {{jalink|Miyanoura-dake|Q10950373}}. === History === The circumstances of its founding are unknown. In the {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}, an entry dated April 1 of the first year of {{jalink|Emperor Shōmu|Q329761}} ({{jalink|629|Q33258}}) records that a member of the Tabe clan was dispatched to Yaku (Yakushima). Compared with the entry in the same chronicle dated November 23 of the 8th year of {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}} ({{jalink|679|Q23451}}) describing an envoy sent to {{jalink|Tanegashima|Q696228}}, the fact that an envoy was sent to Yakushima some 50 years earlier is thought to demonstrate the island’s importance as a target along the southern sea routes. Furthermore, in the {{jalink|Shoku Nihongi|Q611399}}, an entry dated February 20 of the 6th year of {{jalink|Tenpyō-shōhō|Q1373169}} ({{jalink|754|Q23646}}) records that in the 7th year of {{jalink|Tenpyō|Q1199879}} ({{jalink|735|Q23576}}), {{jalink|Takahashi no Muraji Ushikai}} was sent to the southern islands and made to erect markers recording island names, ports, and springs to prepare for the drift landings of {{jalink|Japanese missions to Tang China|Q262529|envoy ships}}. One view holds that the shrine’s alternative name “Sukui” derives from a mistaken kun-reading of the character 救 (“rescue”) in Yaku (益救)Hatta Tomonori et al., *Satsugu Nichi Chiri Sanko*, Kagoshima Prefectural Private Education Association, 1898., while another theory in the *Survey Report on Shikinaisha* suggests that it derives from a corruption of “Tsukushi,” referring to such markers, i.e., {{jalink|Miotsukushi|Q11566019}}, and that this term aptly expresses the shrine’s functional origins. From the {{jalink|8th century|Q8086}} to the early {{jalink|9th century|Q8083}}, when Yakushima and Tanegashima formed {{jalink|Tane Province|Q1370581}}, the shrine is said to have been its {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}. In the *Engishiki* Jinmyōchō compiled in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enchō (era)|Q1327274|Enchō}} ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), “Yaku Shrine” is listed under {{jalink|Ōsumi Province|Q981002}}{{jalink|Gomu District|Q11668133}}, which is regarded as its first appearance in historical documents. It was thereby classified as a minor shrine and became the southernmost Shikinaisha. During the {{jalink|Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|Sengoku period}}, Yakushima came under the control of the {{jalink|Tanegashima clan|Q2975383}}, who favored the {{jalink|Hokke sect|Q44733349}}. As a result, the shrine declined and its location became unknown. Religion on Yakushima had been based on the {{jalink|Ritsu school|Q1351551}}, but around the first year of {{jalink|Bunmei (era)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} ({{jalink|1469|Q6566}}) it shifted to the Hokke sect. In addition, when Mount Yaedake ({{jalink|Nagata-dake|Q11550240}}) shook and various strange phenomena occurred, a monk named {{jalink|Nichizō}} visited the island in the 2nd year of {{jalink|Chōkyō|Q968843}} ({{jalink|1488|Q4853}}) and climbed the sacred mountains—referring to Miyanoura-dake, Nagata-dake, {{jalink|Kurio-dake|Q11536028}}, and {{jalink|Kuromi-dake|Q11678042}}—to perform rites to pacify them. This is thought to suggest a conflict between Hokke Buddhism and mountain worship. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, Yakushima became territory of the {{jalink|Satsuma Domain|Q676187}}. In the first year of {{jalink|Jōkyō|Q1146221}} ({{jalink|1684|Q7654}}), Satsuma retainer {{jalink|Machida Magoshichi Tadayuki}} was appointed as magistrate, and upon lamenting the shrine’s ruin, he located its former site and rebuilt the shrine buildings at the present location the following year, Jōkyō 2 ({{jalink|1685|Q7660}}). By that time, old records had already been lost, and apart from {{jalink|Hoori|Q1051960|Hohodemi no Mikoto}}, the identities of the deities were unknown, so Hohodemi no Mikoto, {{jalink|Hohoseri no Mikoto|Q10731395|Hohoseri no Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ebisu|Q1129330|Ebisu}} were enshrined as principal deities, with the present associated deities enshrined in a side hall. In the 3rd year of {{jalink|Bunkyū|Q730266}} ({{jalink|1863|Q7701}}), the Satsuma Domain again constructed shrine buildings and dedicated 50 {{jalink|koku (unit)|Q855048|koku}} of shrine lands. In the 4th year of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} ({{jalink|1871|Q7746}}), Hohoseri no Mikoto and Ebisu were transferred to other shrines, leaving the present enshrined deities. During {{jalink|World War II|Q362}}, on July 15 of {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 20 ({{jalink|1945|Q5240}}), the shrine buildings were destroyed by bombing by the {{jalink|United States military|Q11211}}. To raise funds for reconstruction, part of the precinct land and about 2,000 cedar trees were sold, and the current buildings were erected in Shōwa 29 ({{jalink|1954|Q18581}}). === Shrine Grounds === * Main hall – {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|Three-bay Nagare-zukuri}}. === Mountain Pilgrimage === On Yakushima, during the spring and autumn {{jalink|equinoxes|Q866977}}, groups centered on young men from each village climb the sacred mountains and bring back branches of {{jalink|rhododendron|Q4389817}} as souvenirs. The mountain climbed differs by village, but in any case reaching the mountainous interior of the island, with elevations exceeding 1,800 m, takes 2–3 days, so representatives would make the ascent. Those who remained behind would climb to the fore-mountain (the mountain viewed from their own village) to welcome the representatives, or gather at distant worship sites called moisho, where they would receive rhododendron branches believed to bear the spirits of the gods and display them in the alcoves of their homes. By the late Shōwa period, however, the practice of bringing back rhododendrons had declined from the standpoint of nature conservation. === Location === * Miyano-ura aza Mizuarai-jiri 277, {{jalink|Yakushima Town|Q1350724}}, {{jalink|Kumage District (Kagoshima Prefecture)|Q1132154|Kumage District}}, {{jalink|Kagoshima Prefecture|Q15701}} === Notes === {{Help footnotes}} {{reflist}} === External Links === {{commonscat}} * {{official website}} {{Shinto}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yaku Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ōsumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:鹿児島県の神社]] [[Category:屋久島町の建築物]] [[Category:屋久島町の歴史]] [[Category:大隅国の式内社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:鹿児島県の旧県社]] [[Category:need translation]]

Ippin Hōju Daigongen |Pilgrimage Sites = |Annual Festival = {{jalink|April 29|Q2535}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagoshima|label=Yaku Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Yaku Shrine Torii.jpg|Thumbnail|Torii]] [[File:Yaku jinja shrine.jpg|Thumbnail|Main hall]] '''Yaku Shrine''' (やくじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in {{jalink|Yakushima Town|Q1350724}} ({{jalink|Yakushima|Q242176}}), {{jalink|Kumage District (Kagoshima Prefecture)|Q1132154|Kumage District}}, {{jalink|Kagoshima Prefecture|Q15701}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and under the {{jalink|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}} it was ranked as a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. It is the {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Tane Province|Q1370581}}. Among the shrines listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}} {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}, it is the southernmost. Today it is read “Yaku,” but in the past it was read “Masukui” or “Sukui.” Former names include “Sukui-no-miya” and “Ippin Hōju Daigongen.” === Enshrined Deities === === Principal Deity === * Amatsu-Hikohiko Hohodemi no Mikoto (あまつひこひこほほでみのみこと) *: Known as “Yamasachihiko” in the {{jalink|Myth of Yamasachihiko and Umasachihiko|Q11468336|Sea and Mountain Luck myth}}. === Associated Deities === * {{jalink|Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|Konohana-Sakuya-Hime no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Shiotsuchi no Oji||Shiotsuchi no Oji no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Watatsumi|Q1474187|Toyotama-hiko no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Toyotama-hime|Q2329261|Toyotama-hime no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Tamayori-hime (Hyūga mythology)|Q3082751|Tamayori-hime no Mikoto}} It is thought that the shrine originally enshrined the deities of the three central mountains of Yakushima—{{jalink|Miyanoura-dake|Q10950373}}, {{jalink|Nagata-dake|Q11550240}}, and {{jalink|Kurio-dake|Q11536028}}—and in the past there were distant worship sites for these three mountains throughout the island. Until the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, there were Yaku Shrines bearing the names of their villages in 18 former villagesEdited by Ken’ichi Tanigawa, *The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Vol. 1 Kyushu*, Hakusuisha, April 1984., but today only this shrine in Miyano-ura and the Hara Yaku Shrine in the Hara settlement (Yakushima Town, Hara 721-2) remain. In addition, an inner shrine of this shrine stands on the summit of {{jalink|Miyanoura-dake|Q10950373}}. === History === The circumstances of its founding are unknown. In the {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}, an entry dated April 1 of the first year of {{jalink|Emperor Shōmu|Q329761}} ({{jalink|629|Q33258}}) records that a member of the Tabe clan was dispatched to Yaku (Yakushima). Compared with the entry in the same chronicle dated November 23 of the 8th year of {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}} ({{jalink|679|Q23451}}) describing an envoy sent to {{jalink|Tanegashima|Q696228}}, the fact that an envoy was sent to Yakushima some 50 years earlier is thought to demonstrate the island’s importance as a target along the southern sea routes. Furthermore, in the {{jalink|Shoku Nihongi|Q611399}}, an entry dated February 20 of the 6th year of {{jalink|Tenpyō-shōhō|Q1373169}} ({{jalink|754|Q23646}}) records that in the 7th year of {{jalink|Tenpyō|Q1199879}} ({{jalink|735|Q23576}}), {{jalink|Takahashi no Muraji Ushikai}} was sent to the southern islands and made to erect markers recording island names, ports, and springs to prepare for the drift landings of {{jalink|Japanese missions to Tang China|Q262529|envoy ships}}. One view holds that the shrine’s alternative name “Sukui” derives from a mistaken kun-reading of the character 救 (“rescue”) in Yaku (益救)Hatta Tomonori et al., *Satsugu Nichi Chiri Sanko*, Kagoshima Prefectural Private Education Association, 1898., while another theory in the *Survey Report on Shikinaisha* suggests that it derives from a corruption of “Tsukushi,” referring to such markers, i.e., {{jalink|Miotsukushi|Q11566019}}, and that this term aptly expresses the shrine’s functional origins. From the {{jalink|8th century|Q8086}} to the early {{jalink|9th century|Q8083}}, when Yakushima and Tanegashima formed {{jalink|Tane Province|Q1370581}}, the shrine is said to have been its {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}. In the *Engishiki* Jinmyōchō compiled in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enchō (era)|Q1327274|Enchō}} ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), “Yaku Shrine” is listed under {{jalink|Ōsumi Province|Q981002}}{{jalink|Gomu District|Q11668133}}, which is regarded as its first appearance in historical documents. It was thereby classified as a minor shrine and became the southernmost Shikinaisha. During the {{jalink|Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|Sengoku period}}, Yakushima came under the control of the {{jalink|Tanegashima clan|Q2975383}}, who favored the {{jalink|Hokke sect|Q44733349}}. As a result, the shrine declined and its location became unknown. Religion on Yakushima had been based on the {{jalink|Ritsu school|Q1351551}}, but around the first year of {{jalink|Bunmei (era)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} ({{jalink|1469|Q6566}}) it shifted to the Hokke sect. In addition, when Mount Yaedake ({{jalink|Nagata-dake|Q11550240}}) shook and various strange phenomena occurred, a monk named {{jalink|Nichizō}} visited the island in the 2nd year of {{jalink|Chōkyō|Q968843}} ({{jalink|1488|Q4853}}) and climbed the sacred mountains—referring to Miyanoura-dake, Nagata-dake, {{jalink|Kurio-dake|Q11536028}}, and {{jalink|Kuromi-dake|Q11678042}}—to perform rites to pacify them. This is thought to suggest a conflict between Hokke Buddhism and mountain worship. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, Yakushima became territory of the {{jalink|Satsuma Domain|Q676187}}. In the first year of {{jalink|Jōkyō|Q1146221}} ({{jalink|1684|Q7654}}), Satsuma retainer {{jalink|Machida Magoshichi Tadayuki}} was appointed as magistrate, and upon lamenting the shrine’s ruin, he located its former site and rebuilt the shrine buildings at the present location the following year, Jōkyō 2 ({{jalink|1685|Q7660}}). By that time, old records had already been lost, and apart from {{jalink|Hoori|Q1051960|Hohodemi no Mikoto}}, the identities of the deities were unknown, so Hohodemi no Mikoto, {{jalink|Hohoseri no Mikoto|Q10731395|Hohoseri no Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ebisu|Q1129330|Ebisu}} were enshrined as principal deities, with the present associated deities enshrined in a side hall. In the 3rd year of {{jalink|Bunkyū|Q730266}} ({{jalink|1863|Q7701}}), the Satsuma Domain again constructed shrine buildings and dedicated 50 {{jalink|koku (unit)|Q855048|koku}} of shrine lands. In the 4th year of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} ({{jalink|1871|Q7746}}), Hohoseri no Mikoto and Ebisu were transferred to other shrines, leaving the present enshrined deities. During {{jalink|World War II|Q362}}, on July 15 of {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 20 ({{jalink|1945|Q5240}}), the shrine buildings were destroyed by bombing by the {{jalink|United States military|Q11211}}. To raise funds for reconstruction, part of the precinct land and about 2,000 cedar trees were sold, and the current buildings were erected in Shōwa 29 ({{jalink|1954|Q18581}}). === Shrine Grounds === * Main hall – {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|Three-bay Nagare-zukuri}}. === Mountain Pilgrimage === On Yakushima, during the spring and autumn {{jalink|equinoxes|Q866977}}, groups centered on young men from each village climb the sacred mountains and bring back branches of {{jalink|rhododendron|Q4389817}} as souvenirs. The mountain climbed differs by village, but in any case reaching the mountainous interior of the island, with elevations exceeding 1,800 m, takes 2–3 days, so representatives would make the ascent. Those who remained behind would climb to the fore-mountain (the mountain viewed from their own village) to welcome the representatives, or gather at distant worship sites called moisho, where they would receive rhododendron branches believed to bear the spirits of the gods and display them in the alcoves of their homes. By the late Shōwa period, however, the practice of bringing back rhododendrons had declined from the standpoint of nature conservation. === Location === * Miyano-ura aza Mizuarai-jiri 277, {{jalink|Yakushima Town|Q1350724}}, {{jalink|Kumage District (Kagoshima Prefecture)|Q1132154|Kumage District}}, {{jalink|Kagoshima Prefecture|Q15701}} === Notes === {{Help footnotes}} {{reflist}} === External Links === {{commonscat}} * {{official website}} {{Shinto}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yaku Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ōsumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:鹿児島県の神社]] [[Category:屋久島町の建築物]] [[Category:屋久島町の歴史]] [[Category:大隅国の式内社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:鹿児島県の旧県社]]

In the first year of {{jalink|Jōkyō|Q1146221}} ({{jalink|1684|Q7654}}), Satsuma retainer {{jalink|Machida Magoshichi Tadayuki}} was appointed as magistrate, and upon lamenting the shrine’s ruin, he located its former site and rebuilt the shrine buildings at the present location the following year, Jōkyō 2 ({{jalink|1685|Q7660}}). By that time, old records had already been lost, and apart from {{jalink|Hoori|Q1051960|Hohodemi no Mikoto}}, the identities of the deities were unknown, so Hohodemi no Mikoto, {{jalink|Hohoseri no Mikoto|Q10731395|Hohoseri no Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ebisu|Q1129330|Ebisu}} were enshrined as principal deities, with the present associated deities enshrined in a side hall. In the 3rd year of {{jalink|Bunkyū|Q730266}} ({{jalink|1863|Q7701}}), the Satsuma Domain again constructed shrine buildings and dedicated 50 {{jalink|koku (unit)|Q855048|koku}} of shrine lands. In the 4th year of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} ({{jalink|1871|Q7746}}), Hohoseri no Mikoto and Ebisu were transferred to other shrines, leaving the present enshrined deities. During {{jalink|World War II|Q362}}, on July 15 of {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 20 ({{jalink|1945|Q5240}}), the shrine buildings were destroyed by bombing by the {{jalink|United States military|Q11211}}. To raise funds for reconstruction, part of the precinct land and about 2,000 cedar trees were sold, and the current buildings were erected in Shōwa 29 ({{jalink|1954|Q18581}}). === Shrine Grounds === * Main hall – {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|Three-bay Nagare-zukuri}}. === Mountain Pilgrimage === On Yakushima, during the spring and autumn {{jalink|equinoxes|Q866977}}, groups centered on young men from each village climb the sacred mountains and bring back branches of {{jalink|rhododendron|Q4389817}} as souvenirs. The mountain climbed differs by village, but in any case reaching the mountainous interior of the island, with elevations exceeding 1,800 m, takes 2–3 days, so representatives would make the ascent. Those who remained behind would climb to the fore-mountain (the mountain viewed from their own village) to welcome the representatives, or gather at distant worship sites called moisho, where they would receive rhododendron branches believed to bear the spirits of the gods and display them in the alcoves of their homes. By the late Shōwa period, however, the practice of bringing back rhododendrons had declined from the standpoint of nature conservation. === Location === * Miyano-ura aza Mizuarai-jiri 277, {{jalink|Yakushima Town|Q1350724}}, {{jalink|Kumage District (Kagoshima Prefecture)|Q1132154|Kumage District}}, {{jalink|Kagoshima Prefecture|Q15701}} === Notes === {{Help footnotes}} {{reflist}} === External Links === {{commonscat}} * {{official website}} {{Shinto}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yaku Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ōsumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:鹿児島県の神社]] [[Category:屋久島町の建築物]] [[Category:屋久島町の歴史]] [[Category:大隅国の式内社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:鹿児島県の旧県社]]

Haiden |Location = {{jalink|Kagoshima Prefecture|Q15701}}{{jalink|Kumage District (Kagoshima Prefecture)|Q1132154|Kumage District}}{{jalink|Yakushima Town|Q1350724}} Miyano-ura aza Mizuarai-jiri 277 |Coordinates = {{Wikicoord2dms|30|25|37.70|N|130|34|18.52|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yaku Shrine}} |Deity = {{jalink|Hoori|Q1051960|Amatsu-Hikohiko Hohodemi no Mikoto}} |Rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (Minor)
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = Three-bay {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Other Names = Mitake-gū (Mitsudake-gū)
Sukui-gū
Ippin Hōju Daigongen |Pilgrimage Sites = |Annual Festival = {{jalink|April 29|Q2535}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagoshima|label=Yaku Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Yaku Shrine Torii.jpg|Thumbnail|Torii]] [[File:Yaku jinja shrine.jpg|Thumbnail|Main hall]] '''Yaku Shrine''' (やくじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in {{jalink|Yakushima Town|Q1350724}} ({{jalink|Yakushima|Q242176}}), {{jalink|Kumage District (Kagoshima Prefecture)|Q1132154|Kumage District}}, {{jalink|Kagoshima Prefecture|Q15701}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and under the {{jalink|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}} it was ranked as a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. It is the {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Tane Province|Q1370581}}. Among the shrines listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}} {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}, it is the southernmost. Today it is read “Yaku,” but in the past it was read “Masukui” or “Sukui.” Former names include “Sukui-no-miya” and “Ippin Hōju Daigongen.” === Enshrined Deities === === Principal Deity === * Amatsu-Hikohiko Hohodemi no Mikoto (あまつひこひこほほでみのみこと) *: Known as “Yamasachihiko” in the {{jalink|Myth of Yamasachihiko and Umasachihiko|Q11468336|Sea and Mountain Luck myth}}. === Associated Deities === * {{jalink|Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|Konohana-Sakuya-Hime no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Shiotsuchi no Oji||Shiotsuchi no Oji no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Watatsumi|Q1474187|Toyotama-hiko no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Toyotama-hime|Q2329261|Toyotama-hime no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Tamayori-hime (Hyūga mythology)|Q3082751|Tamayori-hime no Mikoto}} It is thought that the shrine originally enshrined the deities of the three central mountains of Yakushima—{{jalink|Miyanoura-dake|Q10950373}}, {{jalink|Nagata-dake|Q11550240}}, and {{jalink|Kurio-dake|Q11536028}}—and in the past there were distant worship sites for these three mountains throughout the island. Until the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, there were Yaku Shrines bearing the names of their villages in 18 former villagesEdited by Ken’ichi Tanigawa, *The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Vol. 1 Kyushu*, Hakusuisha, April 1984., but today only this shrine in Miyano-ura and the Hara Yaku Shrine in the Hara settlement (Yakushima Town, Hara 721-2) remain. In addition, an inner shrine of this shrine stands on the summit of {{jalink|Miyanoura-dake|Q10950373}}. === History === The circumstances of its founding are unknown. In the {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}, an entry dated April 1 of the first year of {{jalink|Emperor Shōmu|Q329761}} ({{jalink|629|Q33258}}) records that a member of the Tabe clan was dispatched to Yaku (Yakushima). Compared with the entry in the same chronicle dated November 23 of the 8th year of {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}} ({{jalink|679|Q23451}}) describing an envoy sent to {{jalink|Tanegashima|Q696228}}, the fact that an envoy was sent to Yakushima some 50 years earlier is thought to demonstrate the island’s importance as a target along the southern sea routes. Furthermore, in the {{jalink|Shoku Nihongi|Q611399}}, an entry dated February 20 of the 6th year of {{jalink|Tenpyō-shōhō|Q1373169}} ({{jalink|754|Q23646}}) records that in the 7th year of {{jalink|Tenpyō|Q1199879}} ({{jalink|735|Q23576}}), {{jalink|Takahashi no Muraji Ushikai}} was sent to the southern islands and made to erect markers recording island names, ports, and springs to prepare for the drift landings of {{jalink|Japanese missions to Tang China|Q262529|envoy ships}}. One view holds that the shrine’s alternative name “Sukui” derives from a mistaken kun-reading of the character 救 (“rescue”) in Yaku (益救)Hatta Tomonori et al., *Satsugu Nichi Chiri Sanko*, Kagoshima Prefectural Private Education Association, 1898., while another theory in the *Survey Report on Shikinaisha* suggests that it derives from a corruption of “Tsukushi,” referring to such markers, i.e., {{jalink|Miotsukushi|Q11566019}}, and that this term aptly expresses the shrine’s functional origins. From the {{jalink|8th century|Q8086}} to the early {{jalink|9th century|Q8083}}, when Yakushima and Tanegashima formed {{jalink|Tane Province|Q1370581}}, the shrine is said to have been its {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}. In the *Engishiki* Jinmyōchō compiled in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enchō (era)|Q1327274|Enchō}} ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), “Yaku Shrine” is listed under {{jalink|Ōsumi Province|Q981002}}{{jalink|Gomu District|Q11668133}}, which is regarded as its first appearance in historical documents. It was thereby classified as a minor shrine and became the southernmost Shikinaisha. During the {{jalink|Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|Sengoku period}}, Yakushima came under the control of the {{jalink|Tanegashima clan|Q2975383}}, who favored the {{jalink|Hokke sect|Q44733349}}. As a result, the shrine declined and its location became unknown. Religion on Yakushima had been based on the {{jalink|Ritsu school|Q1351551}}, but around the first year of {{jalink|Bunmei (era)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} ({{jalink|1469|Q6566}}) it shifted to the Hokke sect. In addition, when Mount Yaedake ({{jalink|Nagata-dake|Q11550240}}) shook and various strange phenomena occurred, a monk named {{jalink|Nichizō}} visited the island in the 2nd year of {{jalink|Chōkyō|Q968843}} ({{jalink|1488|Q4853}}) and climbed the sacred mountains—referring to Miyanoura-dake, Nagata-dake, {{jalink|Kurio-dake|Q11536028}}, and {{jalink|Kuromi-dake|Q11678042}}—to perform rites to pacify them. This is thought to suggest a conflict between Hokke Buddhism and mountain worship. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, Yakushima became territory of the {{jalink|Satsuma Domain|Q676187}}. In the first year of {{jalink|Jōkyō|Q1146221}} ({{jalink|1684|Q7654}}), Satsuma retainer {{jalink|Machida Magoshichi Tadayuki}} was appointed as magistrate, and upon lamenting the shrine’s ruin, he located its former site and rebuilt the shrine buildings at the present location the following year, Jōkyō 2 ({{jalink|1685|Q7660}}). By that time, old records had already been lost, and apart from {{jalink|Hoori|Q1051960|Hohodemi no Mikoto}}, the identities of the deities were unknown, so Hohodemi no Mikoto, {{jalink|Hohoseri no Mikoto|Q10731395|Hohoseri no Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ebisu|Q1129330|Ebisu}} were enshrined as principal deities, with the present associated deities enshrined in a side hall. In the 3rd year of {{jalink|Bunkyū|Q730266}} ({{jalink|1863|Q7701}}), the Satsuma Domain again constructed shrine buildings and dedicated 50 {{jalink|koku (unit)|Q855048|koku}} of shrine lands. In the 4th year of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} ({{jalink|1871|Q7746}}), Hohoseri no Mikoto and Ebisu were transferred to other shrines, leaving the present enshrined deities. During {{jalink|World War II|Q362}}, on July 15 of {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 20 ({{jalink|1945|Q5240}}), the shrine buildings were destroyed by bombing by the {{jalink|United States military|Q11211}}. To raise funds for reconstruction, part of the precinct land and about 2,000 cedar trees were sold, and the current buildings were erected in Shōwa 29 ({{jalink|1954|Q18581}}). === Shrine Grounds === * Main hall – {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|Three-bay Nagare-zukuri}}. === Mountain Pilgrimage === On Yakushima, during the spring and autumn {{jalink|equinoxes|Q866977}}, groups centered on young men from each village climb the sacred mountains and bring back branches of {{jalink|rhododendron|Q4389817}} as souvenirs. The mountain climbed differs by village, but in any case reaching the mountainous interior of the island, with elevations exceeding 1,800 m, takes 2–3 days, so representatives would make the ascent. Those who remained behind would climb to the fore-mountain (the mountain viewed from their own village) to welcome the representatives, or gather at distant worship sites called moisho, where they would receive rhododendron branches believed to bear the spirits of the gods and display them in the alcoves of their homes. By the late Shōwa period, however, the practice of bringing back rhododendrons had declined from the standpoint of nature conservation. === Location === * Miyano-ura aza Mizuarai-jiri 277, {{jalink|Yakushima Town|Q1350724}}, {{jalink|Kumage District (Kagoshima Prefecture)|Q1132154|Kumage District}}, {{jalink|Kagoshima Prefecture|Q15701}} === Notes === {{Help footnotes}} {{reflist}} === External Links === {{commonscat}} * {{official website}} {{Shinto}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yaku Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yaku Shrine]][[Category:Pages]] [[Category:ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ōsumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:鹿児島県の神社]] [[Category:屋久島町の建築物]] [[Category:屋久島町の歴史]] [[Category:大隅国の式内社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:鹿児島県の旧県社]]

Haiden |Location = {{jalink|Kagoshima Prefecture|Q15701}}{{jalink|Kumage District (Kagoshima Prefecture)|Q1132154|Kumage District}}{{jalink|Yakushima Town|Q1350724}} Miyano-ura aza Mizuarai-jiri 277 |Coordinates = {{Wikicoord2dms|30|25|37.70|N|130|34|18.52|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yaku Shrine}} |Deity = {{jalink|Hoori|Q1051960|Amatsu-Hikohiko Hohodemi no Mikoto}} |Rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (Minor)
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = Three-bay {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Other Names = Mitake-gū (Mitsudake-gū)
Sukui-gū
Ippin Hōju Daigongen |Pilgrimage Sites = |Annual Festival = {{jalink|April 29|Q2535}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagoshima|label=Yaku Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Yaku Shrine Torii.jpg|Thumbnail|Torii]] [[File:Yaku jinja shrine.jpg|Thumbnail|Main hall]] '''Yaku Shrine''' (やくじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in {{jalink|Yakushima Town|Q1350724}} ({{jalink|Yakushima|Q242176}}), {{jalink|Kumage District (Kagoshima Prefecture)|Q1132154|Kumage District}}, {{jalink|Kagoshima Prefecture|Q15701}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and under the {{jalink|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}} it was ranked as a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. It is the {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Tane Province|Q1370581}}. Among the shrines listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}} {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Register of Deities}}, it is the southernmost. Today it is read “Yaku,” but in the past it was read “Masukui” or “Sukui.” Former names include “Sukui-no-miya” and “Ippin Hōju Daigongen.” === Enshrined Deities === === Principal Deity === * Amatsu-Hikohiko Hohodemi no Mikoto (あまつひこひこほほでみのみこと) *: Known as “Yamasachihiko” in the {{jalink|Myth of Yamasachihiko and Umasachihiko|Q11468336|Sea and Mountain Luck myth}}. === Associated Deities === * {{jalink|Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|Konohana-Sakuya-Hime no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Shiotsuchi no Oji||Shiotsuchi no Oji no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Watatsumi|Q1474187|Toyotama-hiko no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Toyotama-hime|Q2329261|Toyotama-hime no Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Tamayori-hime (Hyūga mythology)|Q3082751|Tamayori-hime no Mikoto}} It is thought that the shrine originally enshrined the deities of the three central mountains of Yakushima—{{jalink|Miyanoura-dake|Q10950373}}, {{jalink|Nagata-dake|Q11550240}}, and {{jalink|Kurio-dake|Q11536028}}—and in the past there were distant worship sites for these three mountains throughout the island. Until the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, there were Yaku Shrines bearing the names of their villages in 18 former villagesEdited by Ken’ichi Tanigawa, *The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Vol. 1 Kyushu*, Hakusuisha, April 1984., but today only this shrine in Miyano-ura and the Hara Yaku Shrine in the Hara settlement (Yakushima Town, Hara 721-2) remain. In addition, an inner shrine of this shrine stands on the summit of {{jalink|Miyanoura-dake|Q10950373}}. === History === The circumstances of its founding are unknown. In the {{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}, an entry dated April 1 of the first year of {{jalink|Emperor Shōmu|Q329761}} ({{jalink|629|Q33258}}) records that a member of the Tabe clan was dispatched to Yaku (Yakushima). Compared with the entry in the same chronicle dated November 23 of the 8th year of {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}} ({{jalink|679|Q23451}}) describing an envoy sent to {{jalink|Tanegashima|Q696228}}, the fact that an envoy was sent to Yakushima some 50 years earlier is thought to demonstrate the island’s importance as a target along the southern sea routes. Furthermore, in the {{jalink|Shoku Nihongi|Q611399}}, an entry dated February 20 of the 6th year of {{jalink|Tenpyō-shōhō|Q1373169}} ({{jalink|754|Q23646}}) records that in the 7th year of {{jalink|Tenpyō|Q1199879}} ({{jalink|735|Q23576}}), {{jalink|Takahashi no Muraji Ushikai}} was sent to the southern islands and made to erect markers recording island names, ports, and springs to prepare for the drift landings of {{jalink|Japanese missions to Tang China|Q262529|envoy ships}}. One view holds that the shrine’s alternative name “Sukui” derives from a mistaken kun-reading of the character 救 (“rescue”) in Yaku (益救)Hatta Tomonori et al., *Satsugu Nichi Chiri Sanko*, Kagoshima Prefectural Private Education Association, 1898., while another theory in the *Survey Report on Shikinaisha* suggests that it derives from a corruption of “Tsukushi,” referring to such markers, i.e., {{jalink|Miotsukushi|Q11566019}}, and that this term aptly expresses the shrine’s functional origins. From the {{jalink|8th century|Q8086}} to the early {{jalink|9th century|Q8083}}, when Yakushima and Tanegashima formed {{jalink|Tane Province|Q1370581}}, the shrine is said to have been its {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}. In the *Engishiki* Jinmyōchō compiled in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enchō (era)|Q1327274|Enchō}} ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), “Yaku Shrine” is listed under {{jalink|Ōsumi Province|Q981002}}{{jalink|Gomu District|Q11668133}}, which is regarded as its first appearance in historical documents. It was thereby classified as a minor shrine and became the southernmost Shikinaisha. During the {{jalink|Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|Sengoku period}}, Yakushima came under the control of the {{jalink|Tanegashima clan|Q2975383}}, who favored the {{jalink|Hokke sect|Q44733349}}. As a result, the shrine declined and its location became unknown. Religion on Yakushima had been based on the {{jalink|Ritsu school|Q1351551}}, but around the first year of {{jalink|Bunmei (era)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} ({{jalink|1469|Q6566}}) it shifted to the Hokke sect. In addition, when Mount Yaedake ({{jalink|Nagata-dake|Q11550240}}) shook and various strange phenomena occurred, a monk named {{jalink|Nichizō}} visited the island in the 2nd year of {{jalink|Chōkyō|Q968843}} ({{jalink|1488|Q4853}}) and climbed the sacred mountains—referring to Miyanoura-dake, Nagata-dake, {{jalink|Kurio-dake|Q11536028}}, and {{jalink|Kuromi-dake|Q11678042}}—to perform rites to pacify them. This is thought to suggest a conflict between Hokke Buddhism and mountain worship. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, Yakushima became territory of the {{jalink|Satsuma Domain|Q676187}}. In the first year of {{jalink|Jōkyō|Q1146221}} ({{jalink|1684|Q7654}}), Satsuma retainer {{jalink|Machida Magoshichi Tadayuki}} was appointed as magistrate, and upon lamenting the shrine’s ruin, he located its former site and rebuilt the shrine buildings at the present location the following year, Jōkyō 2 ({{jalink|1685|Q7660}}). By that time, old records had already been lost, and apart from {{jalink|Hoori|Q1051960|Hohodemi no Mikoto}}, the identities of the deities were unknown, so Hohodemi no Mikoto, {{jalink|Hohoseri no Mikoto|Q10731395|Hohoseri no Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ebisu|Q1129330|Ebisu}} were enshrined as principal deities, with the present associated deities enshrined in a side hall. In the 3rd year of {{jalink|Bunkyū|Q730266}} ({{jalink|1863|Q7701}}), the Satsuma Domain again constructed shrine buildings and dedicated 50 {{jalink|koku (unit)|Q855048|koku}} of shrine lands. In the 4th year of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} ({{jalink|1871|Q7746}}), Hohoseri no Mikoto and Ebisu were transferred to other shrines, leaving the present enshrined deities. During {{jalink|World War II|Q362}}, on July 15 of {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 20 ({{jalink|1945|Q5240}}), the shrine buildings were destroyed by bombing by the {{jalink|United States military|Q11211}}. To raise funds for reconstruction, part of the precinct land and about 2,000 cedar trees were sold, and the current buildings were erected in Shōwa 29 ({{jalink|1954|Q18581}}). === Shrine Grounds === * Main hall – {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|Three-bay Nagare-zukuri}}. === Mountain Pilgrimage === On Yakushima, during the spring and autumn {{jalink|equinoxes|Q866977}}, groups centered on young men from each village climb the sacred mountains and bring back branches of {{jalink|rhododendron|Q4389817}} as souvenirs. The mountain climbed differs by village, but in any case reaching the mountainous interior of the island, with elevations exceeding 1,800 m, takes 2–3 days, so representatives would make the ascent. Those who remained behind would climb to the fore-mountain (the mountain viewed from their own village) to welcome the representatives, or gather at distant worship sites called moisho, where they would receive rhododendron branches believed to bear the spirits of the gods and display them in the alcoves of their homes. By the late Shōwa period, however, the practice of bringing back rhododendrons had declined from the standpoint of nature conservation. === Location === * Miyano-ura aza Mizuarai-jiri 277, {{jalink|Yakushima Town|Q1350724}}, {{jalink|Kumage District (Kagoshima Prefecture)|Q1132154|Kumage District}}, {{jalink|Kagoshima Prefecture|Q15701}} === Notes === {{Help footnotes}} {{reflist}} === External Links === {{commonscat}} * {{official website}} {{Shinto}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yaku Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ōsumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:鹿児島県の神社]] [[Category:屋久島町の建築物]] [[Category:屋久島町の歴史]] [[Category:大隅国の式内社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:鹿児島県の旧県社]]

It is the {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}} of {{ill|lt=Tane Province|Q1370581|WD=Q1370581}}. Among the shrines listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Deities}}, it is the southernmost. Today it is read “Yaku,” but in the past it was read “Masukui” or “Sukui.” Former names include “Sukui-no-miya” and “Ippin Hōju Daigongen.” === Enshrined Deities === === Principal Deity === * Amatsu-Hikohiko Hohodemi no Mikoto (あまつひこひこほほでみのみこと) *: Known as “Yamasachihiko” in the {{ill|lt=Myth of Yamasachihiko and Umasachihiko|Q11468336|WD=Q11468336|lt=Sea and Mountain Luck myth}}. === Associated Deities === * {{ill|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohana-Sakuya-Hime no Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Shiotsuchi no Oji||WD=|lt=Shiotsuchi no Oji no Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Watatsumi|Q1474187|WD=Q1474187|lt=Toyotama-hiko no Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Toyotama-hime|Q2329261|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime no Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Tamayori-hime (Hyūga mythology)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayori-hime no Mikoto}} It is thought that the shrine originally enshrined the deities of the three central mountains of Yakushima—{{ill|lt=Miyanoura-dake|Q10950373|WD=Q10950373}}, {{ill|lt=Nagata-dake|Q11550240|WD=Q11550240}}, and {{ill|lt=Kurio-dake|Q11536028|WD=Q11536028}}—and in the past there were distant worship sites for these three mountains throughout the island. Until the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, there were Yaku Shrines bearing the names of their villages in 18 former villagesEdited by Ken’ichi Tanigawa, *The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Vol. 1 Kyushu*, Hakusuisha, April 1984., but today only this shrine in Miyano-ura and the Hara Yaku Shrine in the Hara settlement (Yakushima Town, Hara 721-2) remain. In addition, an inner shrine of this shrine stands on the summit of {{ill|lt=Miyanoura-dake|Q10950373|WD=Q10950373}}. === History === The circumstances of its founding are unknown. In the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}}, an entry dated April 1 of the first year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Shōmu|Q329761|WD=Q329761}} ({{ill|lt=629|Q33258|WD=Q33258}}) records that a member of the Tabe clan was dispatched to Yaku (Yakushima). Compared with the entry in the same chronicle dated November 23 of the 8th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181}} ({{ill|lt=679|Q23451|WD=Q23451}}) describing an envoy sent to {{ill|lt=Tanegashima|Q696228|WD=Q696228}}, the fact that an envoy was sent to Yakushima some 50 years earlier is thought to demonstrate the island’s importance as a target along the southern sea routes. Furthermore, in the {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399}}, an entry dated February 20 of the 6th year of {{ill|lt=Tenpyō-shōhō|Q1373169|WD=Q1373169}} ({{ill|lt=754|Q23646|WD=Q23646}}) records that in the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Tenpyō|Q1199879|WD=Q1199879}} ({{ill|lt=735|Q23576|WD=Q23576}}), {{ill||lt=Takahashi no Muraji Ushikai|UNKNOWN}} was sent to the southern islands and made to erect markers recording island names, ports, and springs to prepare for the drift landings of {{ill|lt=Japanese missions to Tang China|Q262529|WD=Q262529|lt=envoy ships}}. One view holds that the shrine’s alternative name “Sukui” derives from a mistaken kun-reading of the character 救 (“rescue”) in Yaku (益救)Hatta Tomonori et al., *Satsugu Nichi Chiri Sanko*, Kagoshima Prefectural Private Education Association, 1898., while another theory in the *Survey Report on Shikinaisha* suggests that it derives from a corruption of “Tsukushi,” referring to such markers, i.e., {{ill|lt=Miotsukushi|Q11566019|WD=Q11566019}}, and that this term aptly expresses the shrine’s functional origins. From the {{ill|lt=8th century|Q8086|WD=Q8086}} to the early {{ill|lt=9th century|Q8083|WD=Q8083}}, when Yakushima and Tanegashima formed {{ill|lt=Tane Province|Q1370581|WD=Q1370581}}, the shrine is said to have been its {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}}. In the *Engishiki* Jinmyōchō compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), “Yaku Shrine” is listed under {{ill|lt=Ōsumi Province|Q981002|WD=Q981002}}{{ill|lt=Gomu District|Q11668133|WD=Q11668133}}, which is regarded as its first appearance in historical documents. It was thereby classified as a minor shrine and became the southernmost Shikinaisha. During the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, Yakushima came under the control of the {{ill|lt=Tanegashima clan|Q2975383|WD=Q2975383}}, who favored the {{ill|lt=Hokke sect|Q44733349|WD=Q44733349}}. As a result, the shrine declined and its location became unknown. Religion on Yakushima had been based on the {{ill|lt=Ritsu school|Q1351551|WD=Q1351551}}, but around the first year of {{ill|lt=Bunmei (era)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei}} ({{ill|lt=1469|Q6566|WD=Q6566}}) it shifted to the Hokke sect. In addition, when Mount Yaedake ({{ill|lt=Nagata-dake|Q11550240|WD=Q11550240}}) shook and various strange phenomena occurred, a monk named {{ill||lt=Nichizō|UNKNOWN}} visited the island in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Chōkyō|Q968843|WD=Q968843}} ({{ill|lt=1488|Q4853|WD=Q4853}}) and climbed the sacred mountains—referring to Miyanoura-dake, Nagata-dake, {{ill|lt=Kurio-dake|Q11536028|WD=Q11536028}}, and {{ill|lt=Kuromi-dake|Q11678042|WD=Q11678042}}—to perform rites to pacify them. This is thought to suggest a conflict between Hokke Buddhism and mountain worship. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, Yakushima became territory of the {{ill|lt=Satsuma Domain|Q676187|WD=Q676187}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Jōkyō|Q1146221|WD=Q1146221}} ({{ill|lt=1684|Q7654|WD=Q7654}}), Satsuma retainer {{ill||lt=Machida Magoshichi Tadayuki|UNKNOWN}} was appointed as magistrate, and upon lamenting the shrine’s ruin, he located its former site and rebuilt the shrine buildings at the present location the following year, Jōkyō 2 ({{ill|lt=1685|Q7660|WD=Q7660}}). By that time, old records had already been lost, and apart from {{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hohodemi no Mikoto}}, the identities of the deities were unknown, so Hohodemi no Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Hohoseri no Mikoto|Q10731395|WD=Q10731395|lt=Hohoseri no Mikoto}}, and {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu}} were enshrined as principal deities, with the present associated deities enshrined in a side hall. In the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Bunkyū|Q730266|WD=Q730266}} ({{ill|lt=1863|Q7701|WD=Q7701}}), the Satsuma Domain again constructed shrine buildings and dedicated 50 {{ill|lt=koku (unit)|Q855048|WD=Q855048|lt=koku}} of shrine lands. In the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} ({{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|WD=Q7746}}), Hohoseri no Mikoto and Ebisu were transferred to other shrines, leaving the present enshrined deities. During {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362}}, on July 15 of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}}), the shrine buildings were destroyed by bombing by the {{ill|lt=United States military|Q11211|WD=Q11211}}. To raise funds for reconstruction, part of the precinct land and about 2,000 cedar trees were sold, and the current buildings were erected in Shōwa 29 ({{ill|lt=1954|Q18581|WD=Q18581}}). === Shrine Grounds === * Main hall – {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=Three-bay Nagare-zukuri}}. === Mountain Pilgrimage === On Yakushima, during the spring and autumn {{ill|lt=equinoxes|Q866977|WD=Q866977}}, groups centered on young men from each village climb the sacred mountains and bring back branches of {{ill|lt=rhododendron|Q4389817|WD=Q4389817}} as souvenirs. The mountain climbed differs by village, but in any case reaching the mountainous interior of the island, with elevations exceeding 1,800 m, takes 2–3 days, so representatives would make the ascent. Those who remained behind would climb to the fore-mountain (the mountain viewed from their own village) to welcome the representatives, or gather at distant worship sites called moisho, where they would receive rhododendron branches believed to bear the spirits of the gods and display them in the alcoves of their homes. By the late Shōwa period, however, the practice of bringing back rhododendrons had declined from the standpoint of nature conservation. === Location === * Miyano-ura aza Mizuarai-jiri 277, {{ill|lt=Yakushima Town|Q1350724|WD=Q1350724}}, {{ill|lt=Kumage District (Kagoshima Prefecture)|Q1132154|WD=Q1132154|lt=Kumage District}}, {{ill|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|Q15701|WD=Q15701}} === Notes === {{Help footnotes}} {{reflist}} === External Links === {{commonscat}} * {{official website}} {{Shinto}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yaku Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ōsumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:鹿児島県の神社]] [[Category:屋久島町の建築物]] [[Category:屋久島町の歴史]] [[Category:大隅国の式内社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:鹿児島県の旧県社]]

During the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, Yakushima came under the control of the {{ill|lt=Tanegashima clan|Q2975383|WD=Q2975383}}, who favored the {{ill|lt=Hokke sect|Q44733349|WD=Q44733349}}. As a result, the shrine declined and its location became unknown. Religion on Yakushima had been based on the {{ill|lt=Ritsu school|Q1351551|WD=Q1351551}}, but around the first year of {{ill|lt=Bunmei (era)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei}} ({{ill|lt=1469|Q6566|WD=Q6566}}) it shifted to the Hokke sect. In addition, when Mount Yaedake ({{ill|lt=Nagata-dake|Q11550240|WD=Q11550240}}) shook and various strange phenomena occurred, a monk named {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nichizō|UNKNOWN}} visited the island in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Chōkyō|Q968843|WD=Q968843}} ({{ill|lt=1488|Q4853|WD=Q4853}}) and climbed the sacred mountains—referring to Miyanoura-dake, Nagata-dake, {{ill|lt=Kurio-dake|Q11536028|WD=Q11536028}}, and {{ill|lt=Kuromi-dake|Q11678042|WD=Q11678042}}—to perform rites to pacify them. This is thought to suggest a conflict between Hokke Buddhism and mountain worship. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, Yakushima became territory of the {{ill|lt=Satsuma Domain|Q676187|WD=Q676187}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Jōkyō|Q1146221|WD=Q1146221}} ({{ill|lt=1684|Q7654|WD=Q7654}}), Satsuma retainer {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Machida Magoshichi Tadayuki|UNKNOWN}} was appointed as magistrate, and upon lamenting the shrine’s ruin, he located its former site and rebuilt the shrine buildings at the present location the following year, Jōkyō 2 ({{ill|lt=1685|Q7660|WD=Q7660}}). By that time, old records had already been lost, and apart from {{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hohodemi no Mikoto}}, the identities of the deities were unknown, so Hohodemi no Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Hohoseri no Mikoto|Q10731395|WD=Q10731395|lt=Hohoseri no Mikoto}}, and {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu}} were enshrined as principal deities, with the present associated deities enshrined in a side hall. In the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Bunkyū|Q730266|WD=Q730266}} ({{ill|lt=1863|Q7701|WD=Q7701}}), the Satsuma Domain again constructed shrine buildings and dedicated 50 {{ill|lt=koku (unit)|Q855048|WD=Q855048|lt=koku}} of shrine lands. In the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} ({{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|WD=Q7746}}), Hohoseri no Mikoto and Ebisu were transferred to other shrines, leaving the present enshrined deities. During {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362}}, on July 15 of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}}), the shrine buildings were destroyed by bombing by the {{ill|lt=United States military|Q11211|WD=Q11211}}. To raise funds for reconstruction, part of the precinct land and about 2,000 cedar trees were sold, and the current buildings were erected in Shōwa 29 ({{ill|lt=1954|Q18581|WD=Q18581}}). === Shrine Grounds === * Main hall – {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=Three-bay Nagare-zukuri}}. === Mountain Pilgrimage === On Yakushima, during the spring and autumn {{ill|lt=equinoxes|Q866977|WD=Q866977}}, groups centered on young men from each village climb the sacred mountains and bring back branches of {{ill|lt=rhododendron|Q4389817|WD=Q4389817}} as souvenirs. The mountain climbed differs by village, but in any case reaching the mountainous interior of the island, with elevations exceeding 1,800 m, takes 2–3 days, so representatives would make the ascent. Those who remained behind would climb to the fore-mountain (the mountain viewed from their own village) to welcome the representatives, or gather at distant worship sites called moisho, where they would receive rhododendron branches believed to bear the spirits of the gods and display them in the alcoves of their homes. By the late Shōwa period, however, the practice of bringing back rhododendrons had declined from the standpoint of nature conservation. === Location === * Miyano-ura aza Mizuarai-jiri 277, {{ill|lt=Yakushima Town|Q1350724|WD=Q1350724}}, {{ill|lt=Kumage District (Kagoshima Prefecture)|Q1132154|WD=Q1132154|lt=Kumage District}}, {{ill|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|Q15701|WD=Q15701}} === Notes === {{Help footnotes}} {{reflist}} === External Links === {{commonscat}} * {{official website}} {{Shinto}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yaku Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yaku Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ōsumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:鹿児島県の神社]] [[Category:屋久島町の建築物]] [[Category:屋久島町の歴史]] [[Category:大隅国の式内社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:鹿児島県の旧県社]]
