Sukui-gū
Ippin Hōju Daigongen |Pilgrimage Sites = |Annual Festival = {{ill|lt=April 29|Q2535|WD=Q2535|1=April 29}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagoshima|label=Yaku Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Yaku Shrine Torii.jpg|Thumbnail|Torii]] [[File:Yaku jinja shrine.jpg|Thumbnail|Main hall]] '''Yaku Shrine''' (やくじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Yakushima Town|Q1350724|WD=Q1350724|1=Yakushima, Kagoshima}} ({{ill|lt=Yakushima|Q242176|WD=Q242176|1=Yakushima}}), {{ill|lt=Kumage District (Kagoshima Prefecture)|Q1132154|WD=Q1132154|lt=Kumage District|1=Kumage District, Kagoshima}}, {{ill|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|Q15701|WD=Q15701|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is the {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}} of {{ill|lt=Tane Province|Q1370581|WD=Q1370581|1=Tane Province}}. Among the shrines listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Deities|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it is the southernmost. Today it is read “Yaku,” but in the past it was read “Masukui” or “Sukui.” Former names include “Sukui-no-miya” and “Ippin Hōju Daigongen.” === Enshrined Deities === === Principal Deity === * Amatsu-Hikohiko Hohodemi no Mikoto (あまつひこひこほほでみのみこと) *: Known as “Yamasachihiko” in the {{ill|lt=Myth of Yamasachihiko and Umasachihiko|Q11468336|WD=Q11468336|lt=Sea and Mountain Luck myth|1=Umisachi and Yamasachi}}. === Associated Deities === * {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|1=Ōyamatsumi}} * {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohana-Sakuya-Hime no Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Shiotsuchi no Oji|UNKNOWN|lt=Shiotsuchi no Oji no Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Watatsumi|Q1474187|WD=Q1474187|lt=Toyotama-hiko no Mikoto|1=Watatsumi}} * {{ill|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime|Q2329261|WD=Q2329261|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime no Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Tamayori-hime (Hyūga mythology)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayori-hime no Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} It is thought that the shrine originally enshrined the deities of the three central mountains of Yakushima—{{ill|lt=Miyanoura-dake|Q10950373|WD=Q10950373|1=Mount Miyanoura}}, {{ill|lt=Nagata-dake|Q11550240|WD=Q11550240|1=Mount Nagata (Japan)}}, and {{ill|lt=Kurio-dake|Q11536028|WD=Q11536028|1=Mount Kurio}}—and in the past there were distant worship sites for these three mountains throughout the island. Until the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, there were Yaku Shrines bearing the names of their villages in 18 former villagesEdited by Ken’ichi Tanigawa, *The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Vol. 1 Kyushu*, Hakusuisha, April 1984., but today only this shrine in Miyano-ura and the Hara Yaku Shrine in the Hara settlement (Yakushima Town, Hara 721-2) remain. In addition, an inner shrine of this shrine stands on the summit of {{ill|lt=Miyanoura-dake|Q10950373|WD=Q10950373|1=Mount Miyanoura}}. === History === The circumstances of its founding are unknown. In the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}, an entry dated April 1 of the first year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Shōmu|Q329761|WD=Q329761|1=Emperor Jomei}} ({{ill|lt=629|Q33258|WD=Q33258|1=629}}) records that a member of the Tabe clan was dispatched to Yaku (Yakushima). Compared with the entry in the same chronicle dated November 23 of the 8th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181|1=Emperor Tenmu}} ({{ill|lt=679|Q23451|WD=Q23451|1=AD 679}}) describing an envoy sent to {{ill|lt=Tanegashima|Q696228|WD=Q696228|1=Tanegashima}}, the fact that an envoy was sent to Yakushima some 50 years earlier is thought to demonstrate the island’s importance as a target along the southern sea routes. Furthermore, in the {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}, an entry dated February 20 of the 6th year of {{ill|lt=Tenpyō-shōhō|Q1373169|WD=Q1373169|1=Tenpyō-shōhō}} ({{ill|lt=754|Q23646|WD=Q23646|1=754}}) records that in the 7th year of {{ill|lt=Tenpyō|Q1199879|WD=Q1199879|1=Tenpyō}} ({{ill|lt=735|Q23576|WD=Q23576|1=735}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takahashi no Muraji Ushikai|1=UNKNOWN}} was sent to the southern islands and made to erect markers recording island names, ports, and springs to prepare for the drift landings of {{ill|lt=Japanese missions to Tang China|Q262529|WD=Q262529|lt=envoy ships|1=Japanese missions to Tang China}}. One view holds that the shrine’s alternative name “Sukui” derives from a mistaken kun-reading of the character 救 (“rescue”) in Yaku (益救)Hatta Tomonori et al., *Satsugu Nichi Chiri Sanko*, Kagoshima Prefectural Private Education Association, 1898., while another theory in the *Survey Report on Shikinaisha* suggests that it derives from a corruption of “Tsukushi,” referring to such markers, i.e., {{ill|lt=Miotsukushi|Q11566019|WD=Q11566019|1=miotsukushi}}, and that this term aptly expresses the shrine’s functional origins. From the {{ill|lt=8th century|Q8086|WD=Q8086|1=8th century}} to the early {{ill|lt=9th century|Q8083|WD=Q8083|1=9th century}}, when Yakushima and Tanegashima formed {{ill|lt=Tane Province|Q1370581|WD=Q1370581|1=Tane Province}}, the shrine is said to have been its {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}}. In the *Engishiki* Jinmyōchō compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), “Yaku Shrine” is listed under {{ill|lt=Ōsumi Province|Q981002|WD=Q981002|1=Ōsumi Province}}{{ill|lt=Gomu District|Q11668133|WD=Q11668133|1=Gomu district}}, which is regarded as its first appearance in historical documents. It was thereby classified as a minor shrine and became the southernmost Shikinaisha. During the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, Yakushima came under the control of the {{ill|lt=Tanegashima clan|Q2975383|WD=Q2975383|1=Tanegashima clan}}, who favored the {{ill|lt=Hokke sect|Q44733349|WD=Q44733349|1=Hokke-shū}}. As a result, the shrine declined and its location became unknown. Religion on Yakushima had been based on the {{ill|lt=Ritsu school|Q1351551|WD=Q1351551|1=Risshū (Buddhism)}}, but around the first year of {{ill|lt=Bunmei (era)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} ({{ill|lt=1469|Q6566|WD=Q6566|1=1469}}) it shifted to the Hokke sect. In addition, when Mount Yaedake ({{ill|lt=Nagata-dake|Q11550240|WD=Q11550240|1=Mount Nagata (Japan)}}) shook and various strange phenomena occurred, a monk named {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nichizō|1=UNKNOWN}} visited the island in the 2nd year of {{ill|lt=Chōkyō|Q968843|WD=Q968843|1=Chōkyō}} ({{ill|lt=1488|Q4853|WD=Q4853|1=1488}}) and climbed the sacred mountains—referring to Miyanoura-dake, Nagata-dake, {{ill|lt=Kurio-dake|Q11536028|WD=Q11536028|1=Mount Kurio}}, and {{ill|lt=Kuromi-dake|Q11678042|WD=Q11678042|1=Mount Kuromi}}—to perform rites to pacify them. This is thought to suggest a conflict between Hokke Buddhism and mountain worship. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, Yakushima became territory of the {{ill|lt=Satsuma Domain|Q676187|WD=Q676187|1=Satsuma Domain}}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Jōkyō|Q1146221|WD=Q1146221|1=Jōkyō}} ({{ill|lt=1684|Q7654|WD=Q7654|1=1684}}), Satsuma retainer {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Machida Magoshichi Tadayuki|1=UNKNOWN}} was appointed as magistrate, and upon lamenting the shrine’s ruin, he located its former site and rebuilt the shrine buildings at the present location the following year, Jōkyō 2 ({{ill|lt=1685|Q7660|WD=Q7660|1=1685}}). By that time, old records had already been lost, and apart from {{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hohodemi no Mikoto|1=Hoori}}, the identities of the deities were unknown, so Hohodemi no Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Hohoseri no Mikoto|Q10731395|WD=Q10731395|lt=Hohoseri no Mikoto|1=Hosuseri}}, and {{ill|lt=Ebisu|Q1129330|WD=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu|1=Ebisu (mythology)}} were enshrined as principal deities, with the present associated deities enshrined in a side hall. In the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Bunkyū|Q730266|WD=Q730266|1=Bunkyū}} ({{ill|lt=1863|Q7701|WD=Q7701|1=1863}}), the Satsuma Domain again constructed shrine buildings and dedicated 50 {{ill|lt=koku (unit)|Q855048|WD=Q855048|lt=koku|1=Koku}} of shrine lands. In the 4th year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} ({{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|WD=Q7746|1=1871}}), Hohoseri no Mikoto and Ebisu were transferred to other shrines, leaving the present enshrined deities. During {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|WD=Q362|1=World War II}}, on July 15 of {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}}), the shrine buildings were destroyed by bombing by the {{ill|lt=United States military|Q11211|WD=Q11211|1=United States Armed Forces}}. To raise funds for reconstruction, part of the precinct land and about 2,000 cedar trees were sold, and the current buildings were erected in Shōwa 29 ({{ill|lt=1954|Q18581|WD=Q18581|1=1954}}). === Shrine Grounds === * Main hall – {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|lt=Three-bay Nagare-zukuri|1=Nagare-zukuri}}. === Mountain Pilgrimage === On Yakushima, during the spring and autumn {{ill|lt=equinoxes|Q866977|WD=Q866977|1=Higan}}, groups centered on young men from each village climb the sacred mountains and bring back branches of {{ill|lt=rhododendron|Q4389817|WD=Q4389817|1=Rhododendron subg. Hymenanthes}} as souvenirs. The mountain climbed differs by village, but in any case reaching the mountainous interior of the island, with elevations exceeding 1,800 m, takes 2–3 days, so representatives would make the ascent. Those who remained behind would climb to the fore-mountain (the mountain viewed from their own village) to welcome the representatives, or gather at distant worship sites called moisho, where they would receive rhododendron branches believed to bear the spirits of the gods and display them in the alcoves of their homes. By the late Shōwa period, however, the practice of bringing back rhododendrons had declined from the standpoint of nature conservation. === Location === * Miyano-ura aza Mizuarai-jiri 277, {{ill|lt=Yakushima Town|Q1350724|WD=Q1350724|1=Yakushima, Kagoshima}}, {{ill|lt=Kumage District (Kagoshima Prefecture)|Q1132154|WD=Q1132154|lt=Kumage District|1=Kumage District, Kagoshima}}, {{ill|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|Q15701|WD=Q15701|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}} === Notes === {{Help footnotes}} {{reflist}} === External Links === {{commonscat}} * {{official website}} {{Shinto}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yaku Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yaku Shrine]][[Category:ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ōsumi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kagoshima Prefecture]] [[Category:屋久島町の建築物]] [[Category:屋久島町の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ōsumi Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Kagoshima Prefecture]]
